question_title: Will the community prediction be higher than 65.00% on 2026-02-28 for the Metaculus question "Will an AI model reach a 3 hour time horizon with 80% reliability during 2026?"?
summary: Meta-prediction question asking whether the CP for "Will an AI model reach a 3 hour time horizon with 80% reliability during 2026?" will be strictly above 65.00% on Feb 28. The CP is currently sitting at exactly 0.65 — the threshold — and has been there for ~2 days. It has never been above 0.65 in the recent measurement window (Feb 15-18), oscillating between 0.62-0.65. The status quo resolves NO since 0.65 is not strictly higher than 0.65. However, the underlying trend has been strongly upward (0.45 → 0.65 over 3 weeks), driven by new AI model releases and METR updates. METR hasn't yet measured the newest models (Opus 4.6, GPT-5.3-Codex), and if published, results could push CP higher. With 795 forecasters and 10 days until resolution, this is genuinely close to a coin flip with a slight lean toward NO.

factors.description: CP is exactly at threshold (0.65) — status quo resolves NO since strictly higher is required

10-day historical CP changes show slight positive mean drift (+0.011) with 51% positive changes
Auto-generated threshold was set near current CP — structurally designed as a balanced coin-flip question

**Key reasoning:**

**Critical observations from CP history:**
- CP rose dramatically from ~0.45 (mid-Jan) to 0.65 (mid-Feb), driven by Opus 4.6/GPT-5.3-Codex releases and METR updates
- The CP has stabilized at exactly 0.65 for the last 2 days and has **never been above 0.65** in the Feb 15-18 window
- 795 forecasters provide a stable, hard-to-move base

**For YES:** Strong upward momentum, continuing AI news cycle, METR hasn't yet measured the newest models (estimates suggest ~8hr at 50% reliability), and fundamental case for the underlying question keeps strengthening.

**For NO:** CP has equilibrated at the threshold, large forecaster base resists movement, and no guaranteed catalyst in the 10-day window.

The slight lean toward NO (48% vs 50%) reflects that the status quo outcome is NO — the CP needs to move *up* from its current resting point.
